title: feat(block;backend):  Truncate execution update payload on large data & Improve ReadSpreadsheetBlock performance
body:
### Changes 🏗️

This PR introduces several key improvements to message handling, block functionality, and execution reliability:

- **Renamed CSV block to Spreadsheet block** with enhanced CSV/Excel processing capabilities
- **Added message size limiting and truncation** for Redis communication to prevent connection issues
- **Optimized FileReadBlock** to yield content chunks instead of duplicated outputs for better performance
- **Improved execution termination handling** with better timeout management and event publishing
- **Enhanced continuous retry decorator** with async function support
- **Implemented payload truncation** to prevent Redis connection issues from oversized messages

### Checklist 📋

#### For code changes:
- [x] I have clearly listed my changes in the PR description
- [x] I have made a test plan
- [x] I have tested my changes according to the test plan:
  - [x] Verified backend starts without errors
  - [x] Confirmed message truncation works for large payloads
  - [x] Tested spreadsheet block functionality with CSV and Excel files
  - [x] Validated execution termination improvements
  - [x] Checked FileReadBlock chunk processing

#### For configuration changes:
- [x] `.env.example` is updated or already compatible with my changes
- [x] `docker-compose.yml` is updated or already compatible with my changes
- [x] I have included a list of my configuration changes in the PR description (under **Changes**)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

step_plan.summary: Add Redis execution update payload truncation to prevent oversized pub/sub messages, fix termination end-time handling, improve graph finalization updates, add async-safe retry behavior, and optimize the Spreadsheet (CSV/Excel) reading block to stream data efficiently.

steps.operation: Add payload-size limiting + truncation to node execution update publishing (sync bus) before Redis publish; keep schema valid and attach truncation metadata
steps.reason: Node execution updates can include very large input/output blobs; publishing full `model_dump()` payloads risks exceeding Redis client/message limits and causing connection instability. Truncation must occur at event creation so all publishers are protected consistently.
steps.side_effects: Downstream subscribers may receive truncated fields (e.g., large strings/lists/dicts) and should rely on metadata/flags for awareness. Requires adding/using a shared helper and may need new/updated tests asserting size bounds and JSON validity.

steps.operation: Apply the same payload-size limiting + truncation policy to graph execution update publishing (sync bus)
steps.reason: Graph-level updates can carry even larger aggregated execution data; the graph publisher must enforce the same max payload size to prevent oversized Redis messages.
steps.side_effects: Terminal graph updates may omit large nested execution data, which could change observability for consumers that expected full payloads. Ensure event_type and required identifiers remain intact.

steps.operation: Apply payload-size limiting + truncation to node execution update publishing (async bus), using the same helper and size thresholds
steps.reason: Async execution paths must not bypass truncation; otherwise large payloads can still break Redis communication when published from async workers.
steps.side_effects: Async call sites will now pay a small serialization/size-check cost before publish. Add coverage to ensure async publisher behavior matches sync behavior (including metadata flags).

steps.operation: Apply payload-size limiting + truncation to graph execution update publishing (async bus), matching sync behavior
steps.reason: Ensures consistent protection from oversized messages regardless of whether the graph updates are emitted from sync or async execution contexts.
steps.side_effects: Same as sync graph updates; ensure truncation logic is deterministic to avoid flaky tests and that the async publisher still publishes reliably under timeout/termination scenarios.

steps.operation: Fix termination transition data: treat TERMINATED as terminal by setting endedTime (and any other terminal fields) in status update payloads
steps.reason: Execution termination is a first-class status, but without endedTime the system can leave executions appearing “open” to downstream consumers, breaking duration metrics, cleanup logic, and UI state.
steps.side_effects: May change persisted execution records for terminated runs (endedTime now present). Downstream logic that inferred “running” from missing endedTime may need adjustment; add/adjust tests for TERMINATED status transitions.

steps.operation: Adjust graph execution stats update gating to allow final writes/events on termination/timeouts (as appropriate), not only during RUNNING/QUEUED
steps.reason: If stats/status updates are blocked once state leaves RUNNING/QUEUED, termination flows can miss final state propagation or final counters/timestamps. This complements the TERMINATED fix and improves reliability for timeouts/forced stops.
steps.side_effects: Potentially increases frequency of graph execution writes at the end of runs; ensure idempotency and avoid double-finalization. Verify subscribers still receive exactly one coherent terminal update event.

steps.operation: Enhance continuous_retry decorator to support coroutine functions: use async wrapper with asyncio.sleep while preserving existing sync behavior
steps.reason: The current sync-only implementation uses time.sleep, which would block the event loop if applied to async functions. Supporting async ensures retry logic is safe and consistent across sync/async call sites.
steps.side_effects: Behavioral differences in retry timing may surface in async contexts (e.g., cancellation semantics). Requires adding/adjusting tests to cover both sync and async decorated functions and ensure retries stop/propagate exceptions as expected.

steps.operation: Optimize spreadsheet reading by streaming row outputs rather than materializing full datasets; fix skip_columns handling to match declared types and avoid unnecessary duplication of outputs
steps.reason: Current implementation can be memory/time heavy for large CSV/Excel files by building full `rows` in memory and yielding duplicated structures. Streaming per-row (and capping/optional aggregation) improves performance and avoids large intermediate payloads.
steps.side_effects: May change block output contract if `rows` is no longer always fully materialized; ensure backward compatibility via an option (e.g., cap rows, toggle aggregate output) or maintain existing keys with bounded content. Update block tests and any dependent graphs expecting full `rows`.
